---
title: "Why Meta Stopped Being the Truth Police"
date: 2025-01-19
subtitle: "\"The internet is the first thing that humanity has built that humanity doesn't understand.\" — Eric Schmidt"
description: "In January 2025, Meta announced that it was ending its fact-checking programme on Facebook, Instagram, and Threads. Instead, it would shift to a 'community notes' model, similar to what X (formerly Twitter) uses. Mark Zuckerberg framed it as a free speech move. Critics called it an abdication of responsibility. Who is right?"
image: "./title_20250119.png"
twitter-card: {image: "./title_20250119.png"}
open-graph: {image: "./title_20250119.png"}
categories: ["Corporate Actions"]
author: "Satvik Raman"
---
<p><b>Pageviews:</b> <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="1em" height="1em" viewBox="0 0 256 256"><path fill="currentColor" d="M247.31 124.76c-.35-.79-8.82-19.58-27.65-38.41C194.57 61.26 162.88 48 128 48S61.43 61.26 36.34 86.35C17.51 105.18 9 124 8.69 124.76a8 8 0 0 0 0 6.5c.35.79 8.82 19.57 27.65 38.4C61.43 194.74 93.12 208 128 208s66.57-13.26 91.66-38.34c18.83-18.83 27.3-37.61 27.65-38.4a8 8 0 0 0 0-6.5M128 168a40 40 0 1 1 40-40a40 40 0 0 1-40 40"/></svg> <span class="waline-pageview-count"></i><p>

# Introduction

In early January 2025, Mark Zuckerberg announced a significant change in how Meta would manage information on its platforms. The company would be ending its third-party fact-checking programme, which had been in place since 2016 and employed independent organisations to review and label content flagged as potentially false or misleading. In its place, Meta would introduce a system called Community Notes, similar to the approach used by X, the platform formerly known as Twitter, after Elon Musk took it over in 2022. Under Community Notes, ordinary users can add context and corrections to posts they believe are misleading, with those notes displayed if a sufficient number of users from diverse perspectives agree that the note is helpful and accurate. Zuckerberg framed the move as a correction of overreach, arguing that the fact-checking programme had suppressed too much content and had introduced a political bias into content moderation. Critics, including many organisations that had participated in the fact-checking network, argued that abandoning professional fact-checkers would accelerate the spread of false information during elections and public health crises. Understanding who is likely to be right requires looking at how both systems actually work in practice.

# How Fact-Checking Worked and Why It Was Controversial

Meta's third-party fact-checking programme worked by contracting with dozens of organisations, which included media outlets, academic institutions, and specialist research groups. These organisations employed professional fact-checkers whose job was to identify content circulating widely on Facebook and Instagram that appeared to be factually wrong or misleading. When a piece of content was fact-checked and found to be false or partly false, Meta would apply a label to it explaining the verdict, and the platform's algorithm would significantly reduce how widely that content was distributed. The content was not removed, but its reach was dramatically curtailed. Meta claimed this approach reduced resharing of fact-checked content by approximately eighty percent. The programme attracted criticism from two opposite directions. From the political right in the United States, there were consistent allegations that the fact-checking organisations disproportionately flagged content aligned with conservative viewpoints. From researchers and public health advocates, there were criticisms that the programme was too slow and inconsistent to meaningfully address the volume of false information spreading across Meta's platforms, which reach approximately three billion people.

# How Community Notes Differs

Community Notes operates on a fundamentally different philosophy. Rather than relying on the judgement of a designated expert, it relies on the collective judgement of a broad community of users. The critical design feature is the requirement for cross-partisan agreement: a note is only displayed if contributors with demonstrably different political viewpoints both agree that the note is accurate and helpful. This is designed to prevent the notes from being hijacked by any particular ideological group. The model originated at Twitter and has shown some promise in controlled studies. A 2022 study found that users who saw Community Notes on a piece of misinformation were significantly less likely to share it than users who saw a professional fact-checker label on the same content. However, the model also has clear limitations. Notes are far slower to appear than professional fact-checks, because they depend on enough diverse users seeing, agreeing on, and submitting a note. In the hours when a false story is spreading fastest, Community Notes is often absent.

# What This Means for India

Meta's platforms, particularly Facebook and WhatsApp, are among the most significant information infrastructure in India. India has approximately four hundred million Facebook users, making it the platform's largest national market. WhatsApp, also owned by Meta, is the dominant messaging platform for a vast number of Indians. The consequences of changes to how information spreads on these platforms are not abstract. False information about health, religion, and politics has demonstrably led to violence in India in the past, and the platforms have been criticised by Indian authorities and civil society groups for not doing enough to address the problem. Whether Community Notes, built largely around an English-language, American-user base, can function effectively in the linguistically and culturally diverse context of India is a genuine empirical question that the policy change does not answer.

# Final Thoughts

The debate about Meta's fact-checking decision ultimately comes back to a question about who should decide what is true and who should bear the consequences of getting that wrong. Professional fact-checkers have expertise, independence, and accountability. They also have limitations, biases, and finite capacity. Crowds can be wise in aggregate and resistant to manipulation by a single actor. They can also amplify harmful consensus views and be too slow or indifferent to correct obscure but important falsehoods. Neither model is clearly superior to the other in all circumstances, and the honest answer is that no one fully knows whether the shift to Community Notes will improve or worsen the quality of information available to Meta's billions of users. That uncertainty, at scale, affecting billions of people's information environment, is a reasonable cause for concern.
